title: W2: Data Types
text:
This week’s workshop we’re going to start working together in groups.
Pick one of you to be the ‘driver’. They will type the code. The rest of you are navigators, you need to tell the driver where to go (i.e., what to type!).
Driver instructions:

Log in to the bigger screen at your table
Open RStudio Server
Listen to your navigators

Navigator instructions:

Tell your driver where to go (i.e., what to type)!



Question 1


Open a new R script for this week.
The first thing we’re going to do is to get some practice with packages.

load the “tidyverse” (it should be already installed on the server)
install and load the “skimr” package







Hints





For help, check out [LINK P2 flashcard].








Solution



Solution 1. To load the tidyverse, we just need to do:

library(tidyverse)

To install the “skimr” package, we need to use install.packages("skimr"). BUT, we probably don’t want to keep this in our script, because then it would re-install the package every time we ran the whole script.
Installing packages is one of the few things we might type specifically in the console

install.packages("skimr")

And then back in our script we will want:

library(skimr)





Question 2


Now that we’ve loaded packages, we want to read in some data!
Last week, we put a little survey up for you to fill out, so that we could have data for you to play with.
The data are available at: TODO LINK
Read it in to your R session now. Be sure to assign it a name!






Hints





Here’s the basic usage of read_csv() and how you can assign its output to a variable named mydata.
Replace the ... with the actual location of the data (the location could be somewhere stored on your computer, or the URL address of where it is located online!)

mydata <- read_csv("...")









Solution



Solution 2. 

sdata <- read_csv("../../data/usmr2025.csv")





Question 3


How many people do we have data for? And on how many variables?






Hints





Functions like dim(data), nrow(data), ncol(data) will come in handy here.
You can also try using the skim(data) function (from the “skimr” package we installed a minute ago) — it will give you loads of information!








Solution



Solution 3. 




Question 4


Go through each variable and write down what type of variable it is (e.g., is it continuous, categorical, or ordinal?)






Hints





See flashcard S4 TODO link








Solution



Solution 4. 




Question 5


R will be default treat a variable with only numbers in it as a numeric variable, and anything else as a “character” variable (“character” = any letters/numbers/symbols).
Change the eye colour variable to a factor in R, that only has the options “brown”, “blue”, “green” or “hazel”.
What happens when you now do skim(data)? How does it treat this variable differently?






Hints





Here is the basic code structure for taking a dataframe named mydata and adding a new variable (or changing an existing one).

Line 2 takes the data, and passes it to line 3.

Lines 3-5 “mutates” the data, adding a variable called new_variable. The new_variable is going to contain the values of an existing variable (old_variable) but with some function applied to them. For example, if we had new_variable = old_variable/2 then it would contain the same values but halved.
It feels odd, but it helps to think about the first line of code once we’ve thought of the others. This line re-assigns the name mydata to the mutated mydata, thereby making the change save to the object in R’s memory.


mydata <-
  mydata |> 
  mutate(
    new_variable = function(old_variable)
  )

To make a variable a factor, the function we want is factor(). This will consider every different value that we have to be a possible category:

factor(variable)

If we want to have a specific set of possible values, we can do so with the levels part of the function, replacing the "..." with the options we consider valid:

factor(variable, levels = c("...","...","...", ....... ))

You can see more of this stuff in Flashcard P3 TODO link.








Solution



Solution 5. 

sdata <-
  sdata |>
  mutate(
    eyecolour = factor(eyecolour, levels = c("brown","blue","green","hazel"))
  )


skim(sdata)

title: W1: Introductions
text:
This week’s workshop is quite minimal. All we’re really wanting you to do is:

Explore the software and start playing around.

Meet some new people, make some new friends, and form a group to work with in these workshops.

From next week, you’ll be working in these groups collaboratively on the workshop questions, with one person doing the typing (the ‘driver’) and the others telling them what to type (the ‘navigators’). The role of driver will swap each week, so you’ll always get a turn at typing.


Question 1


The very first task here is to open RStudio.
R and RStudio is software that you can download and install on your computer, but for the duration of this course, we are providing you with a version on a server.
You can access the RStudio server here: https://rstudio.ppls.ed.ac.uk.
We would recommend you bookmark the page so that you can easily find it again!




Question 2


The first thing to do is start using R just like a calculator.
Try doing some basic calculations by typing in the bottom left where you see the blue > and pressing enter.
Playing with these: *, /, +, -, ^ and seeing what happens - can you work out what each one does?






stuck seeing a + symbol?





If we give R an incomplete request, then it will just stay waiting for it to be finished.
e.g., if we typed a query and missed a bracket, then R would just leave a + symbol to say “I’m waiting for more…”:

To get out of this we can either type the required stuff to finish the query, or, much more simply, just press the Escape key (Esc) and it will cancel the query and let you start it again.







Question 3


In order to save our work, we need to start writing our commands in a document. Something we can edit, save, come back to later on, and share with other people.

Open an R Script (File > New File > R Script).

A new window will pop up in the top left of RStudio. Write some calculations in there (put each one on a new line).

You’ll notice that writing in the script doesn’t actually perform the calculations. We need to “Run” the commands we are writing, which we can do by ensuring the cursor is on the line we want to run, and then either:


press Ctrl+Enter (or Cmd+Enter for Mac)
clicking the “Run” button in the top right of the R Script.





Question 4



my_name <- "Umberto"
about_me <- "Italian."
fun_fact <- "I can play the piano."

paste("Hello, my name is", my_name, 
      "and I am", about_me,
      "Fun fact:", fun_fact)


Copy-Paste the lines of code below into your R Script and edit them to provide some text about yourself (if you don’t want to share a fun fact about yourself, just share a random fun fact!)
Save your script (File > Save).
Close your script (there’s a little cross in the tab label at the top of the script)
Re-open your script (File > Open)
Celebrate!





Question 5


TODO need fix once group naming conventions fixed.
Finally, we’d like you to join your group on Blackboard Learn.
Groups are named based on the workshop (1, 2, 3, 4) and your table number.
So if this is Workshop 3, and you are sitting at table 6, you should join group “WS3.6”.

Go to the course LEARN page
click Groups on the top menu bar.
click Workshops_1_2_3_4
Join your group
Now click on “Group Discussion Space” in the top menu of the course LEARN page.
Upload your script to the discussion space, to share your fun fact with your group members.
